content: The bond dissociation energies for $\mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{I}_2$ and ICl are $242.3,151.0$ and $211.3 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is $62.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of $\operatorname{ICl}(\mathrm{g})$ nearly equal to?
explanation:
To determine the standard enthalpy of formation of ICl(g), we consider the following reaction:

$ \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(g) + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ICl}(g) $

We have been provided with the following data:

Dissociation of Cl$_2$:

$ \mathrm{Cl}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}(g), \quad \Delta H = 242.3 \, \text{kJ/mol} $

Dissociation of I$_2$:

$ \mathrm{I}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}(g), \quad \Delta H = 151 \, \text{kJ/mol} $

Dissociation of ICl:

$ \mathrm{ICl}(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}(g) + \mathrm{Cl}(g), \quad \Delta H = 211.3 \, \text{kJ/mol} $

Enthalpy of Sublimation of I$_2$:

$ \mathrm{I}_2(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_2(g), \quad \Delta H = 62.8 \, \text{kJ/mol} $

To calculate the enthalpy change of the given reaction, we use the following steps:

$ \text{Required enthalpy change} = \frac{1}{2}(\Delta H_{\text{I}_2}) + \frac{1}{2}(\Delta H_{\text{Cl}_2}) - \Delta H_{\text{ICl}} + \frac{1}{2}(\Delta H_{\text{Sublimation}}) $

Substituting the given values, we have:

$ \Delta H = \frac{1}{2}(151) + \frac{1}{2}(242.3) - 211.3 + \frac{1}{2}(62.8) $

Calculating this gives:

$ \Delta H = 16.8 \, \text{kJ/mol} $

Thus, the standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{ICl}(g)$ is approximately $16.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$.

content: The value of $$\Delta E$$ for combustion of $$32 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CH}_4$$ is $$-1770778 \mathrm{~J}$$ at $$298 \mathrm{~K}$$. The $$\Delta H$$ combustion for $$\mathrm{CH}_4$$ in $$\mathrm{J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ at this temperature will be (Given that, $$R=8.314 \mathrm{~JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ )
1 mole of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ gas at $$300 \mathrm{~K}$$ is expanded under adiabatic conditions such that its volume becomes 27 times. What is work done?
explanation:
For adiabatic condition $$\frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{1.33-1}=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{0.33}=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & T_2=300 \times \frac{1}{3}=100 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $$T_2< T_1$$, hence cooling takes place due to expansion under adiabatic condition.

$$\Delta E=q+W=W$$

($$\because q=0$$ for adiabatic change)

Sign of $$\Delta E$$ is negative because the gas expands.

$$\begin{aligned} W & =-\Delta E \\ & =-C_V\left(T_2-T_1\right) \\ & =-6(100-300) \\ & =1200 \mathrm{~cal} \end{aligned}$$

content: In thermodynamics, a quantity whose value simply depends upon the initial and final state of the system is called
explanation: It is a state function property is a state function if the change in its value during a process depends upon initial state and liquid state of the system and does not depend upon the path.

content: If $$\Delta H$$ and $$\Delta S$$ are positive for a reaction, the reaction will be spontaneous only when
explanation:
$$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$$ for spontaneity,

$$\Delta G^{\circ}<0$$ and this occurs when $$T \Delta S>\Delta H$$
